title: Feature Comparison Criteria
concept: Filtering support, hybrid search, scaling model, ecosystem, and operational maturity as selection criteria.

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of using a vector search extension in an existing database instead of a dedicated vector database?

options.text: They are always faster and scale better than dedicated vector databases.
They let you keep your existing database infrastructure and operational tools.
They completely eliminate the need to generate embeddings.

exercises.explain: Using a vector extension on your existing database (like pgvector for PostgreSQL) lets you leverage your existing infrastructure, security, and backups without adding a new system.

cards.title: Beyond Raw Speed
cards.body: If you need to build a production-grade LLM app, how do you pick the right home for your vectors? Speed is great, but real-world apps need filters, hybrid search, and rock-solid reliability to survive in production.

cards.title: The Feature Checklist
cards.body: When choosing a vector database, look beyond speed. Consider filtering support (limiting results by categories), hybrid search (combining keywords and vectors), scaling models, ecosystem tools, and operational maturity.

cards.title: The Delivery Vehicle Decision
cards.body: Choosing a vector database is like buying a delivery vehicle. You don't just look at speed; you check if it has refrigerated storage for fresh food, handles highways and dirt roads, and has mechanics nearby who know how to fix it when it breaks.

cards.title: Real-World Recommendations
cards.body: A streaming app uses a vector database to recommend movies. It needs filtering support to exclude R-rated movies for kids' profiles, and hybrid search to find 'action movies' while matching the exact word 'Batman'.

cards.title: The Speed Trap
cards.body: Many believe the fastest database on a benchmark is always the best choice. In reality, a slightly slower database with robust metadata filtering and great developer tools is much more useful for real apps.

cards.title: Search Comparison
cards.body: Compare how a search changes when you use pure vector search versus a feature-rich hybrid search with filtering.

left.text: Finds conceptually similar items, but might return out-of-stock items or miss exact keyword matches.

right.text: Finds conceptually similar items, filters out unavailable ones, and matches exact product serial numbers.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of 'metadata filtering' in a vector database?

options.text: It speeds up the vector math by reducing the dimensions of the vectors.
It restricts search results to vectors matching specific non-vector criteria like category or date.
It automatically translates foreign languages before embedding them.
It generates new embeddings on the fly for missing data fields.

exercises.explain: Metadata filtering allows you to restrict vector search results based on structured attributes, such as showing only items that are 'in_stock' or matching a specific 'user_id'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A vector database's ecosystem integration with frameworks like LangChain can significantly reduce your development time.
exercises.explain: Ecosystem support (like integrations with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and popular cloud providers) makes it much faster and easier to build and deploy your application.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the search method that merges keyword matching and vector matching.
exercises.sentence: To find documents using both exact keywords and conceptual meaning, you should look for a database that supports hybrid search.
exercises.explain: Hybrid search combines the semantic understanding of vectors with the exact matching of keyword search to give you the best of both worlds.

exercises.prompt: Identify the explanation that sounds like it was written by an AI instead of a practical software developer.

options.text: We chose our database because it has a Python SDK that easily plugs into LangChain, saving us weeks of integration work.
This architectural paradigm facilitates the simultaneous execution of sparse and dense retrieval mechanisms, thereby optimizing recall metrics.
Our team went with a mature, hosted service because we didn't want to manage sharding and server backups ourselves.

exercises.explain: Option B is clearly AI-generated because it uses overly formal, robotic phrasing ('This architectural paradigm facilitates the simultaneous execution...') to describe a standard developer choice.

exercises.prompt: You are building an e-commerce chatbot that needs to find products by concept (semantic search), exact SKU codes (keyword search), and only show items in stock (filtering). Which combination of criteria is most critical for this vector database selection?

options.text: Maximum dimensional scaling and raw vector floating-point precision
Metadata filtering, hybrid keyword/semantic search, and strong ecosystem integration
Pure vector similarity speed, ignoring metadata and keyword capabilities
Low-level C++ compilation options and custom hardware acceleration only

exercises.explain: Hybrid search combines keyword and semantic search, metadata filtering narrows down the initial pool, and ecosystem integration ensures it plugs into tools like LangChain or LlamaIndex. These are the three pillars of feature matching for LLM apps.
